narrative_blocks.header: Brickmaker, postmaster, JP, state representative
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Stiles built a multi-role public life in North Haven Connecticut — farmer and brick manufacturer, Justice of the Peace and Postmaster for many years, warden of St. John's Episcopal Church, and representative of North Haven in the Lower House of the Connecticut General Assembly in 1831. He 'held all important town offices' per the Stiles family book — typical of the local-elite figure who structured pre-Civil-War Connecticut town life.

narrative_blocks.header: Two young sons died 1832, probable epidemic
narrative_blocks.body: Two of Isaac Stiles's documented six children — Edward C. and William H. — both died in 1832. The shared death year strongly suggests an epidemic; 1832 was the year of the first North American cholera pandemic that reached Connecticut. Isaac himself died February 4, 1842 at age forty-nine, leaving widow Lois Cooper Stiles to live until 1872 — thirty years past him.

He 'held all important town offices' per the Stiles family book — typical of the local-elite figure who structured pre-Civil-War Connecticut town life.

career.notes: Per Stiles book #953: 'when eighteen years old went to Haddam, Conn., to take charge of a brick yard.'
Brick manufacturing business with his brother Isaac Lorenzo Stiles (Stiles book #950).
Local militia company. The title 'Captain' by which he was ever afterwards designated derived from this command, predating his Civil War commission.
Three terms from 1855.
Resigned in August 1862 to enlist in Civil War.

military_service.rank: Captain (Co. K 15th CT Vol Inf, then Co. B 21st Veteran Reserve Corps)
military_service.unit: 15th Connecticut Volunteer Infantry, Co. K (commission dated Aug 1, 1862); then 21st Veteran Reserve Corps, Co. B (Dec 1863)
military_service.notes: Enlisted Aug 9, 1862 New Haven CT; elected Captain of Co. K, 15th Connecticut Volunteers (commission Aug 1, 1862). Engaged at the Battle of Fredericksburg (December 1862, BTL026), at Edenton Road, Providence Church, and skirmishes near Norfolk Virginia. Honorable discharge for ill health Aug 17, 1863. Returned to service December 1863 as Captain of Co. B, 21st Regiment Veteran Reserve Corps, assigned to Providence Rhode Island as Post Commandant. In March 1864 removed with his company to Albany New York where he commanded the Rendezvous Camp. While there he organized, armed, and equipped the 192nd New York State Volunteers. Acting Post Commissary, Quartermaster, and command of two companies — these arduous duties broke his health again, and he resigned his commission Aug 1, 1865. On separation, his commands presented him with an inscribed sword, a heavy gold-headed cane, a costly jewelled watch and chain, field-glasses, and revolvers.

narrative_blocks.header: Captain at Fredericksburg, organized 192nd New York, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Stiles enlisted at New Haven Aug 9, 1862 and was elected captain of Co. K, 15th Connecticut Volunteers, serving at the Battle of Fredericksburg in December 1862. Discharged for ill health, he returned as captain of the 21st Veteran Reserve Corps and commanded the Rendezvous Camp at Albany New York in 1864, where he organized, armed, and equipped the 192nd New York State Volunteers.

narrative_blocks.header: Knew the end was approaching in fall 1877
narrative_blocks.body: The Stiles Family in America entry 953 records: 'In the fall of 1877, he fully realized that the end of his life was approaching, and prepared his business as he thought best.' He died at North Haven in April 1878 at age fifty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Brick yard at eighteen made the man
narrative_blocks.body: At eighteen in 1842 he went to Haddam Connecticut to take charge of a brick yard. The Stiles book notes that 'the position developed the character that he afterwards sustained through life'; he returned home to partner with brother Isaac Lorenzo as I L Stiles & Son, brickmakers.

narrative_blocks.header: Troops parted with sword, cane, jewelled watch
narrative_blocks.body: When he resigned his commission at Albany in August 1865 from arduous duties as Post Commandant and Quartermaster, his commands presented him with an inscribed sword, a heavy gold-headed cane, a jewelled watch and chain, field-glasses, and revolvers. The 192nd New York Volunteers had been organized, armed, and equipped under his command at the Rendezvous Camp.

sources.label: The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #953

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Julius and Sally Heaton of North Haven CT. Both non-Hooker, not built. IMPORTANT: This Heaton family is DISTINCT from the Eaton/Heaton family of Timothy Eaton + Elizabeth Stiles (H00449/I00505) — that family's surname was corrected to Eaton (Theophilus Eaton, New Haven founder) in Stage 6Y-35. The Julius+Sally Heaton family stays as Heaton. Sarah Jane Heaton's son Edgar Heaton Stiles (HD3927) carries her maiden surname as middle name.

narrative_blocks.header: He returned to the legislature after thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: A New Haven brickmaker and church warden who filled nearly every town office, he sat in the Connecticut house in 1854 — then again in 1884 and 1885, three decades later. He led the firm I. L. Stiles & Son into his late sixties.

narrative_blocks.header: He married a Hooker cousin without the name
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife, Sophronia Blakeslee, turned out to descend from the same Stiles-Hooker root he did, through a different daughter — a cousin match neither surname revealed.

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven bride of a Hooker cousin

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Anson Blakeslee + Julia (Frost) Blakeslee. Per Stiles book #950: 'Julia, dau of Titus and Mabel Frost' — Julia IS our existing H01162 Julia Frost Blakeslee! Sophronia is herself gen 7 Hooker descendant via H01162 → H00448 Mabel Stiles Frost branch. Marriage to Isaac Lorenzo Stiles (HD3934, gen 7 via Mary Stiles H00452 branch) is a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage between two different branches of Esther Hooker → Rev. Isaac Stiles. Sophronia will be RECLASSIFIED to HD-prefix in Stage 6Y-43 after H01162 Julia is enriched with full child list. Built here as I-spouse with cousin_marriage tag.

narrative_blocks.header: Wed Isaac Lorenzo on her 23rd birthday, 1842
narrative_blocks.body: Sophronia Maria Blakeslee was born February 16, 1819 in North Haven Connecticut; on her 23rd birthday — February 16, 1842 — she married Isaac Lorenzo Stiles in the same town. Their union joined two branches of Esther Hooker's descendants: Sophronia descended through the Mabel Stiles Frost line, Isaac Lorenzo through the Mary Stiles Mansfield line.

narrative_blocks.header: She married on her twenty-third birthday
narrative_blocks.body: On February 16, 1842 — her birthday — she wed Isaac Lorenzo Stiles, born the same year as she. The match joined two branches of the Esther Hooker line, a cousin marriage that doubled their sons’ descent.

narrative_blocks.header: Seventeen years a Chapel Street dentist, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Wadsworth Stiles practiced dentistry on New Haven's Chapel Street for seventeen years before dying of heart disease at his West Haven home in February 1907 age 64. Before dentistry he had served as postmaster in North Haven, where his father Isaac Lorenzo Stiles ran the family brick manufacturing company.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on his sixty-fourth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Wadsworth Stiles was born February 28, 1843 and died of heart disease on February 28, 1907 — his sixty-fourth birthday. He had been an invalid for several years before his death at home in West Haven.

sources.label: New Haven Morning Journal and Courier obit, Feb 11, 1907

narrative_blocks.header: He took over his father’s brickyard and seat
narrative_blocks.body: He succeeded his father as president of I. L. Stiles & Son brickmakers and, like him, won a term in the Connecticut house from North Haven in 1902. The Stileses ran the family brick business across two generations.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Elijah Hull and Nancy Hull of North Haven CT per Stiles book #604. Both non-Hooker, not built.

narrative_blocks.header: Edward died young
narrative_blocks.body: Edward died young in 1832, of epidemic.

narrative_blocks.header: William died young
narrative_blocks.body: William died young in 1832. His brother Edward died in the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Late-life politics: Selectman, Assemblyman, then died
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Clark Stiles served as Selectman of North Haven in 1828 and 1829 and was elected to the Connecticut General Assembly in 1831. He died three years later in June 1834 at age sixty-seven.

burialCemetery.name: New Center Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Cemetery in North Haven CT. Major Stiles family burial ground spanning multiple generations — Isaac Stiles (HD3922) + Lois Cooper (I00524); infant son Sterling (HD3933); son Isaac Lorenzo (HD3934). Additional Stiles burials to be added in subsequent stages.
